transcript:
Assistant: What's going on that you'd like help with today?
Parent: My son has been having huge meltdowns every morning before school. He's 7, autistic, nonverbal-ish, uses some signs. It's been happening for about three weeks.
Assistant: That sounds exhausting. Has anything changed recently at school or at home?
Parent: They moved his classroom two weeks ago, and I think the new room is louder. We tried noise-canceling headphones but he won't keep them on.
Assistant: Have you talked to the school about going back to the old room or another option?
Parent: I emailed his teacher but haven't heard back yet. I don't really know what to ask for.

Assistant: What's been the hardest part lately?
Parent: My daughter is 5, has Down syndrome, and she used to sleep through the night but for the last month she's waking up at 2am and won't go back to sleep.
Assistant: Has anything in her routine changed around when this started?
Parent: We did just start a new med for her thyroid around then, actually. I hadn't connected those.
Assistant: That's worth mentioning to her doctor. Have you mentioned the sleep change to them yet?
Parent: No, I haven't called yet. I wasn't sure if it was related or just a phase.

Assistant: What's on your mind today?
Parent: I have an IEP meeting next week for my son and I'm really anxious. Last year I felt like they just told me what they decided and I didn't push back.
Assistant: What would you want to be different this time?
Parent: I want to actually understand what services he's getting and ask for more speech therapy, he's 9 and still has a really hard time being understood.
Assistant: Do you have his current IEP goals written down anywhere you could bring?
Parent: I have the document somewhere but honestly haven't reread it since last year.

Assistant: What's been going on at home?
Parent: My older daughter, she's 10, has been acting out a lot. I think she's jealous of how much attention her younger brother gets, he has cerebral palsy and needs a lot of physical therapy support.
Assistant: Has she said anything directly about how she's feeling?
Parent: A little. She said once that I never come to her stuff anymore. That kind of broke my heart.
Assistant: Have you been able to carve out any one-on-one time with her recently?
Parent: Not really, between his appointments and work it's been hard to find time.

Assistant: What would be most helpful to talk through?
Parent: My son is 4, nonverbal, and lately he's been biting himself when he can't get something across. It's new in the last couple weeks.
Assistant: Has he had any communication tools introduced recently, like a device or picture system?
Parent: We started a basic picture board at home about a month ago but he barely uses it.
Assistant: Has anyone shown you or him how to use it together, like a speech therapist modeling it?
Parent: Not really, the school just sent it home with a one-page instruction sheet.

Assistant: What's happening that brought you here today?
Parent: We just got my daughter's autism diagnosis two days ago, she's 3. I don't even know where to start.
Assistant: That's a lot to take in all at once. Has the team given you any next steps yet?
Parent: They mentioned early intervention but said I have to call myself to set it up and I haven't done it yet, I'm still kind of in shock.
Assistant: That's completely understandable. Is there a specific worry that's loudest for you right now?
Parent: Mostly just whether I'm going to mess this up by not knowing what I'm doing.

Assistant: What's been hardest lately?
Parent: My son is 14, has ADHD and an anxiety disorder, and for the past two weeks he's refusing to go to school, just stays in his room.
Assistant: Has something specific happened at school recently?
Parent: He mentioned getting made fun of in gym class a few weeks ago but didn't want to talk more about it.
Assistant: Have you been able to loop in the school counselor at all?
Parent: I called once and left a voicemail but no callback yet.

Assistant: What's on your mind today?
Parent: Honestly I'm just burned out. My son is 12, has a rare genetic condition, needs help with basically everything, and I haven't had a break in months.
Assistant: Do you have any respite care or family support you can call on?
Parent: My sister offered once but I felt too guilty to take her up on it. I don't know where to even find respite care otherwise.
Assistant: Have you looked into whether your state's Medicaid waiver covers respite hours?
Parent: I've heard the term but have no idea how that process works or if we qualify.
